blog.title: Blog
blog.subtitle: FP&A guides for individuals, finance teams, and wealth managers.

articles.title: How to Build a Personal Budget That Actually Works
articles.description: Most people try to budget and give up within a month. A simple, honest framework for taking control of your money — income, expenses, savings rate, and what to do first.

articles.title: The 13-Week Cash Flow Forecast: Why Every Business Needs One
articles.description: Profit and cash are not the same thing. A 13-week rolling cash forecast is the single most important tool for any business managing liquidity.

articles.title: DCF Valuation Explained: How to Value a Business Using Discounted Cash Flow
articles.description: The gold standard for business valuation — how to build a DCF model, choose a discount rate, and interpret the output correctly.

articles.title: Break-Even Analysis: The First Financial Calculation Every Business Owner Should Know
articles.description: Fixed costs, variable costs, contribution margin, margin of safety — and why every pricing decision should start here.

articles.title: How to Analyse Your Investment Portfolio: Return, Risk and Diversification
articles.description: Most investors track returns. The best investors track risk-adjusted returns. Here's what to measure and why.

articles.title: How to Build a 5-Year Financial Model for Your Business
articles.description: Investors and banks ask for 5-year projections. Here's how to build a three-statement model that actually holds up under scrutiny.

articles.title: How to Build an Annual Budget That Your Finance Team Will Actually Use
articles.description: Most company budgets fail because they're set once and never revisited. Here's how to build one that drives real decisions.

articles.title: Why Financial Forecasting Is the Most Underused Tool in Business
articles.description: Most businesses react to numbers. The best ones anticipate them. Here's why forecasting is the foundation of every sound business decision.

articles.title: Bootstrapping & Runway: How to Extend Your Startup's Life Without Giving Up Equity
articles.description: Every month of runway is a month of optionality. How founders can think rigorously about burn rate and capital efficiency.

articles.title: The UK Pension Puzzle: Why Starting Early Could Mean £200,000 More in Retirement
articles.description: How the UK tax system makes pension contributions one of the most efficient wealth-building tools available.

articles.title: Power BI vs Streamlit: Which One Is Right for Your Finance Team?
articles.description: An honest comparison of two popular analytics tools — pros, cons, and when to use each.

breakEven.title: ⚖️ Break-Even Analysis
breakEven.errorMargin: Selling price must be greater than variable cost per unit to calculate a break-even point.

financialPlanner.title: 🗺️ Financial Journey
financialPlanner.s1WarningOverspend: Your expenses exceed your income by {amount}/mo. Consider reducing spending before investing.
financialPlanner.s1SuccessSavings: Savings rate of {x}% — above the recommended 20% target. Great position to build wealth.
financialPlanner.s2ChartSub: Balance vs estimated interest cost — sorted by size
financialPlanner.s2AvalancheTip: 💡 Debt Avalanche tip: Your highest-rate debt is {debt} at {rate}% APR. Paying this off first minimises total interest paid.
financialPlanner.s3ChartSub: Monthly contributions of {amount} at {rate}% annual return
financialPlanner.s4AllocSub: {profile} profile · Age {age} · adjusted for time horizon
s5BestPractices: Pay yourself first — automate savings on payday
Maximise employer pension match before other investments
Build emergency fund (3-6 months expenses) before investing aggressively
Pay off high-interest debt (above 10%) before investing
Diversify across asset classes and geographies
Review and rebalance your portfolio annually
Increase your savings rate by 1% each year
financialPlanner.s5RecSavingsLow: Your savings rate is {rate}%. Target at least 20%. Review discretionary spending to find room to save more.
financialPlanner.s5RecSavingsOk: Savings rate of {rate}% is a good start. Aim for 20% to accelerate wealth building.
financialPlanner.s5RecSavingsGood: Excellent savings rate of {rate}%. You are on track to build meaningful long-term wealth.
financialPlanner.s5RecEmergencyFund: Keep 3–6 months of expenses ({currency}{amount}) in a liquid savings account before investing aggressively.
financialPlanner.s5RecHighDebt: You have high-interest debt at {rate}%. Prioritise paying this off before increasing investments — it is a guaranteed {rate}% return.
financialPlanner.s5RecDebtHigh: Your total debt is {x}× your annual income. Focus on reducing debt alongside saving.
financialPlanner.s5RecDebtLow: Low debt-to-income ratio — you are in a strong financial position to invest and grow wealth.
financialPlanner.s5RecCompound: Investing {currency}{monthly}/month at {rate}% for {years} years projects to {currency}{final}. Time in market is your biggest advantage.
financialPlanner.s5RecAllocAggressive: High equity allocation suits your risk profile. Rebalance annually and stay the course during market downturns.
financialPlanner.s5RecAllocModerate: Balanced allocation across equities and bonds. Review annually as your age and circumstances change.
financialPlanner.s5RecAllocConservative: Conservative allocation protects capital. Consider increasing equity exposure if your investment timeline allows.

companyPlanner.noteTaxRate: Effective rate applied to pre-tax profit
companyPlanner.chartPLWaterfallSub: Revenue through to Net Profit — each bar shows the running total
companyPlanner.insightEBITDANeg: ⚠️ EBITDA is negative ({currency}{loss} loss). Review your cost structure — OpEx of {currency}{opex} exceeds Gross Profit of {currency}{grossProfit}.
companyPlanner.insightEBITDAHealthy: ✅ Healthy EBITDA margin of {margin}%. Businesses with margins above 15% typically command premium acquisition multiples.
companyPlanner.insightEBITDALow: 🟡 EBITDA margin of {margin}% — positive but below the 15% threshold where buyers typically apply higher multiples. Consider where costs can be optimised.
companyPlanner.insightEBITDAPosNetLoss: 🟡 EBITDA is positive but the business is loss-making after interest and tax. High interest costs or D&A may be suppressing reported profitability.
companyPlanner.chartWCBreakdownSub: Based on your AR / AP / Inventory days and revenue
companyPlanner.chartCFBridgeSub: Operating CF, CapEx drag, debt repayments and net position
companyPlanner.insightFCFNeg: ⚠️ Negative Free Cash Flow of {currency}{amount}. The business is consuming cash after CapEx. Review capital allocation or reduce CapEx spend.
companyPlanner.insightCashConvLow: 🟡 Cash conversion of {pct}% — below the 70–80% range that buyers expect. High working capital or CapEx relative to EBITDA drags cash quality.
companyPlanner.insightCashConvStrong: ✅ Strong cash conversion of {pct}%. Good FCF quality is a key driver of acquisition multiples.
companyPlanner.insightNegEquity: ⚠️ Negative book equity of {currency}{amount}. Total liabilities exceed total assets. This will complicate any sale or refinancing process and requires immediate attention.
companyPlanner.insightLeverageHigh: ⚠️ Leverage of {lev}× is above 3×. This is the typical ceiling for PE-backed acquisitions. Reducing debt would significantly improve exit options.
companyPlanner.insightLeverageGood: ✅ Leverage of {lev}× EBITDA — a conservative capital structure that keeps all exit options open, including PE and trade sale.
companyPlanner.insightLeverageAmber: 🟡 Leverage of {lev}× — approaching the upper end of what most buyers consider manageable. Monitor closely and prioritise debt reduction ahead of any exit.
companyPlanner.noteDCFFCF: FCF grows at 5% p.a. for 5 years in this model.
companyPlanner.exitValueCreated: Value created above book equity: {amount} ({mult}× book value)
companyPlanner.chartValuationSub: Equity value (after net debt) for each method
companyPlanner.insightNegEBITDAValuation: ⚠️ Negative EBITDA means the EV/EBITDA multiple cannot be applied. Focus on reaching EBITDA breakeven before targeting a sale.
companyPlanner.insightBlendedValuation: ℹ️ Multiples used are illustrative — actual market multiples vary significantly by sector, growth rate and deal size. The blended figure ({currency}{amount}) is a starting reference for exit planning, not a formal valuation.
companyPlanner.tradeSaleWhen: Best when EBITDA > 0 and revenue > £500k. Strategic buyers pay synergy-adjusted premiums.
tradeSaleTips: Clean up financials 18–24 months before engaging advisors
Normalise EBITDA — add back owner salary above market rate, one-off costs
Run a competitive process with 3+ strategic buyers to drive price tension
companyPlanner.peBuyoutWhen: Best when EBITDA > £500k, margin > 15%, leverage < 3×. PE needs debt capacity to fund the deal.
peBuyoutTips: Build a management team that can operate without the founder post-close
Demonstrate recurring revenue and low customer churn (NRR > 100% is ideal)
Maintain a 3-year financial track record with clean audit or review
Simplify the cap table — PE hates complex structures with multiple share classes
companyPlanner.mboWhen: Suitable at any size if management has appetite. Works best when FCF is strong enough to service acquisition debt.
mboTips: Arrange debt financing early — typically 3–4× EBITDA from specialist lenders
Align management incentives with EBITDA growth targets via equity ratchets
Keep lender covenants achievable — model a downside case before committing
Engage a specialist MBO solicitor and a deal structuring advisor from day one
companyPlanner.organicGrowthWhen: Always applicable. Reinvesting FCF rather than exiting — builds value ahead of a future sale at higher multiples.
organicGrowthTips: Reinvest FCF into highest-ROI activities — new markets, products or key hires
Manage working capital tightly — every day of AR reduction frees cash
Target 20%+ EBITDA margin before scaling headcount significantly
Build recurring revenue streams — subscription or retainer models command 2–3× higher multiples
companyPlanner.keyActionsTitle: Key Actions to Maximise Exit Price
companyPlanner.actionImproveMargin: Focus on margin improvement — buyers pay premium multiples for EBITDA margins above 15–20%. Review every cost line before entering a process.
companyPlanner.actionReduceDebt: Reduce debt before exit — leverage above 3× EBITDA compresses equity value and makes PE debt financing harder. Target sub-2× for maximum proceeds.
companyPlanner.actionImproveCashConversion: Improve cash conversion — buyers scrutinise the gap between EBITDA and free cash flow. Tighten AR collections and review CapEx spend.
companyPlanner.actionRecapitalise: Recapitalise the balance sheet — negative equity will complicate any sale, refinancing or due diligence process. Address before engaging advisors.
companyPlanner.actionCleanFinancials: Maintain 3 years of clean, audited (or reviewed) financials — this is the single most important factor in a smooth exit process.
companyPlanner.actionDocumentContracts: Document all customer contracts and revenue recognition — recurring, contracted revenue commands the highest multiples in any sale process.
companyPlanner.disclaimerText: This tool is for illustrative and planning purposes only. The valuations and insights above are indicative estimates based on simplified models. They do not constitute financial advice, a formal valuation or an offer to buy or sell any business. Consult a qualified M&A advisor, corporate finance professional or accountant before making any decisions related to a business sale, acquisition or restructuring.
